title: Data-Efficient Indentation Size Effect Correction in Steels Using Machine Learning and Physics-Guided Augmentation
abstract: Shallow nanoindentation enables mechanical characterization of thin films, individual phases and other volume-constrained materials, but measured hardness is often inflated by the indentation size effect (ISE), contact-area errors and tip-geometry artifacts. Classical ISE corrections such as the Nix-Gao require a deep linear regime and are unreliable when only shallow measurements are used. This study investigates how a small experimental dataset can be used to predict a reference hardness with physics-guided feature engineering and augmentation. Approximately 700 experimental indentations were collected from three steel reference specimens covering a hardness range of 2-6.5 GPa and augmented using physically motivated variations representing instrumental noise, session-level drift, and local multiphase boundary blending. The input space combined Oliver-Pharr values with mechanics descriptors, including indentation work partitioning, ($H\text{/}E_{r}$), and the area-invariant compliance proxy ($P_{\max}\text{/}S^{2}$). Ridge Regression (RR), Random Forest, XGBoost, and Neural Networks (NN) were evaluated using a quarantined fourth steel specimen tested at staggered loads. The hardness mapping was nonlinear: RR failed, whereas nonlinear models achieved ($R^2 > 0.98$) internally. A constrained (64-8-64) NN gave the best results, reaching RMSE = 0.470 GPa, MAPE = 5.4% on the quarantined steel. Unlike Nix-Gao analysis, the NN produced stable estimates in the shallow regime. SHAP and latent-space analysis showed reliance on area-invariant and energy-based descriptors. The results demonstrate the feasibility of a this workflow for ISE correction in steels using small datasets and suggest a pathway toward data-efficient characterization of any volume constrained materials.
summary: This paper presents a machine learning approach to correct for measurement errors in shallow nanoindentation of steel. By using physics-guided features and data augmentation, a neural network can accurately predict reference hardness even when classical mechanical models fail. This workflow offers a way to perform efficient mechanical characterization of volume-constrained materials using small datasets.
main_problem: Correcting the indentation size effect (ISE) in shallow nanoindentation of steels, where classical models like Nix-Gao fail due to the lack of a deep linear regime.
main_result: A constrained neural network achieved high predictive accuracy (RMSE = 0.470 GPa, MAPE = 5.4%) for reference hardness, outperforming linear models and providing stable estimates in the shallow regime.
method: Physics-guided feature engineering and data augmentation (simulating noise and drift) applied to a small experimental dataset, using models including Ridge Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and Neural Networks.
model_or_system: The study uses experimental nanoindentation data from three steel reference specimens with hardness ranging from 2-6.5 GPa, supplemented by augmented data representing instrumental noise and multiphase boundary blending.
key_observables: Hardness (H), contact-area errors, indentation work partitioning, H/Er ratio, and the area-invariant compliance proxy (Pmax/S^2).
important_parameters: Indentation depth (shallow regime), hardness range (2-6.5 GPa), and features related to energy-based and area-invariant descriptors.
main_assumptions: The effectiveness of the machine learning model relies on the quality of the physics-guided augmentation and the representativeness of the engineered mechanical descriptors.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ISE in shallow indentation, describes the experimental dataset and augmentation strategy, details the feature engineering and ML model architectures, evaluates performance on a quarantined specimen, and uses SHAP/latent-space analysis to interpret model reliance on physical descriptors.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
